title: A Short History of South Korea
subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 13 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Paleolithic and Neolithic Korea
items.summary: Hunter-gatherers gradually transitioned to farming in the Korean peninsula.
items.text: The earliest humans arrived on the Korean peninsula during the Paleolithic era, leaving behind stone tools and cave paintings. By the Neolithic period, communities developed agriculture, pottery, and dolmen burial structures that still dot the landscape today. These early inhabitants laid the foundation for Bronze Age cultures that would emerge in subsequent millennia. The transition from nomadic hunting to settled farming marked the beginning of Korean civilization's long arc.

items.label: Gojoseon
items.summary: Korea's legendary first kingdom established a Bronze Age civilization in Manchuria and the peninsula.
items.text: According to traditional accounts, Dangun founded Gojoseon in 2333 BC, making it one of the world's oldest kingdoms, though modern historians debate the exact chronology. This kingdom developed a sophisticated bronze culture and established trade networks across East Asia. Gojoseon absorbed multiple waves of migration and developed its own unique governmental systems and spiritual traditions. The kingdom's eventual fall to Chinese Han forces in 108 BC marked the beginning of Chinese influence on the peninsula that would last for centuries.

items.label: Three Kingdoms Era
items.summary: Goguryeo, Baekje, and Silla competed for dominance while Chinese cultural influences flooded the peninsula.
items.text: After Gojoseon's collapse, three major kingdoms emerged: Goguryeo in the north, Baekje in the southwest, and Silla in the southeast, each vying for supremacy. Buddhism arrived during this period and became deeply woven into Korean culture, inspiring magnificent temple architecture and artistic traditions. Chinese administrative systems, writing, and philosophies transformed Korean governance, though each kingdom maintained distinct identities and fierce independence. Constant warfare between the kingdoms created an era of cultural dynamism but also instability, as each power sought regional hegemony.

items.label: Unified Silla
items.summary: Silla conquered its rivals and unified the peninsula, ushering in a golden age of Buddhist culture.
items.text: Silla's military genius and strategic alliances allowed it to absorb Baekje in 660 and Goguryeo in 668, achieving the peninsula's first unified kingdom. Under rulers like Queen Sondok and King Seongdeok, Silla experienced a cultural renaissance that produced exquisite Buddhist temples, advanced astronomical observatories, and thriving arts. The kingdom adopted Tang Chinese administrative models while maintaining Korean cultural identity, creating a sophisticated court society. Silla's prosperity and stability made it a beacon of East Asian civilization, though by the 9th century, regional warlords began fragmenting its authority.

items.label: Goryeo Dynasty
items.summary: A new dynasty reunified Korea and gave the peninsula its modern name through sophisticated governance and artistic achievement.
items.text: Wang Geon founded Goryeo after absorbing the fragmented remains of Silla, restoring unity and establishing a dynasty that would last nearly five centuries. The name Korea itself derives from Goryeo, and the kingdom became renowned for its celadon pottery, Buddhist sculpture, and the invention of woodblock printing and metal movable type. Goryeo developed a civil service examination system based on Confucian ideals, creating a educated bureaucratic class that managed the kingdom with remarkable sophistication. The dynasty weathered Mongol invasions and internal strife, but gradually weakened as Confucianism challenged Buddhism's cultural dominance and military juntas destabilized governance.

items.label: Joseon Dynasty
items.summary: A new Confucian kingdom replaced Buddhism with state ideology and created a cultural golden age that defined Korean civilization for five centuries.
items.text: General Yi Seong-gye founded Joseon after overthrowing the weakened Goryeo, inaugurating the peninsula's longest-lasting dynasty and establishing Confucianism as the state philosophy. Under visionary rulers like Sejong the Great, Joseon achieved remarkable innovations including the Korean alphabet Hangul, advanced military technology, and astronomical breakthroughs. The society became rigidly hierarchical with a yangban aristocratic class dominating politics and culture, while women's rights deteriorated and the caste system hardened. Despite the Imjin War (1592–1598) against Japanese invader Toyotomi Hideyoshi and subsequent Manchu invasions, Joseon recovered and experienced a cultural flowering in the 17th and 18th centuries.

items.label: Joseon Renaissance
items.summary: The dynasty's final prosperous era produced remarkable intellectual movements and practical innovations before Western pressure destabilized the kingdom.
items.text: The 18th century marked Joseon's cultural and intellectual peak, with scholars pursuing practical learning that challenged rigid Neo-Confucian orthodoxy and inspired technological advancement. King Jeongjo patronized the Suwon Fortress and sponsored extensive historical compilations, while merchants and proto-capitalists began challenging the yangban monopoly on wealth and status. Catholic Christianity quietly spread among intellectuals and commoners alike, offering alternative spiritual paths to the established order. However, this flourishing period coincided with growing Western pressure on East Asia, internal factional conflicts, and economic strain that would eventually overwhelm the aging dynasty.

items.label: Forced Opening and Modernization
items.summary: Japan forced Korea open to the modern world and gradually colonized the peninsula through military and economic domination.
items.text: Japan's military pressure compelled Korea to sign the Ganghwa Treaty in 1876, ending centuries of isolation and opening ports to foreign trade and influence. Modernization initiatives clashed with conservative Confucian elites, producing civil unrest including the Donghak Peasant Revolt and triggering Japanese intervention in Korean affairs. The Russo-Japanese War (1904–1905) saw Korea become a Japanese protectorate, with the peninsula's sovereignty systematically stripped away through unequal treaties and military occupation. By 1910, Japan formally annexed Korea, ending the Joseon Dynasty and beginning 35 years of colonial rule marked by cultural suppression and economic exploitation.

items.label: Japanese Colonial Period
items.summary: Japan's brutal colonization modernized Korea's infrastructure while crushing Korean identity and independence aspirations.
items.text: Japanese colonial administrators built railroads, factories, and modern cities while systematically erasing Korean culture through forced assimilation policies including mandatory Japanese language education. The March 1st Movement of 1919 saw mass protests against occupation, brutally suppressed but inspiring nationalist movements that would persist throughout the colonial period. Korean intellectuals, artists, and revolutionaries pursued independence through armed resistance, cultural preservation, and exile movements, with many finding refuge in Manchuria and Shanghai. World War II's end in 1945 finally liberated Korea, but the peninsula immediately faced division by Soviet and American forces, thwarting dreams of immediate reunification.

items.label: Division and the Korean War
items.summary: Korea split into two hostile nations at the 38th parallel, then plunged into a devastating war that killed millions and cemented division.
items.text: Soviet and American military forces divided Korea at the 38th parallel in 1945, establishing separate zones of occupation that quickly hardened into rival states with incompatible ideologies. The Democratic People's Republic of Korea emerged under communist leader Kim Il-sung in the north, while the Republic of Korea formed in the south under authoritarian Syngman Rhee. On June 25, 1950, North Korea invaded the south, triggering the Korean War as United Nations forces, led by the United States, clashed with North Korean and Chinese armies. The three-year conflict killed approximately three million people, devastated both nations, and ended with an armistice that established a demilitarized zone, leaving Korea permanently divided into two separate countries.

items.label: Post-War Division and South Korea's Rise
items.summary: South Korea rebuilt from ashes through authoritarian industrialization, transforming from poor agrarian nation to economic powerhouse.
items.text: After the armistice, South Korea lay in ruins with a devastated economy and traumatized population, yet visionary leaders pursued rapid industrialization through government-directed development. Military strongman Park Chung-hee (1963–1979) implemented five-year economic plans, built export-driven manufacturing, and constructed infrastructure like the Seoul-Busan highway, sacrificing democratic freedoms for economic growth. By the 1970s and 1980s, South Korea's chaebol conglomerates like Samsung and Hyundai achieved global prominence, transforming the nation into an industrial tiger and dramatically raising living standards. This period of authoritarianism and repression also produced vibrant cultural resistance, labor movements, and Christian faith communities that would ultimately demand democratic reform.

items.label: Democratization and Global Emergence
items.summary: South Korea transitioned to democracy, hosted the Olympic Games, and established itself as a cultural and technological leader.
items.text: The June Democratic Struggle of 1987 forced the military government to accept direct presidential elections, ending decades of authoritarianism and initiating South Korea's democratic transformation. The 1988 Seoul Olympics showcased the nation's modernization to the world while massive labor strikes and civil rights movements reshaped society from below. South Korea's entertainment and popular culture industries began capturing global audiences through cinema, music, and television dramas, establishing soft power that complemented its technological prowess. The 1997 Asian financial crisis temporarily shocked the economy but spurred recovery through corporate restructuring and further globalization.

items.label: Contemporary South Korea
items.summary: South Korea became a global technology and culture powerhouse while navigating ongoing tensions with the North and digital transformation.
items.text: The 21st century saw South Korea dominate global technology markets through companies like Samsung, LG, and SK Hynix, while also leading the digital revolution with world-class internet infrastructure and mobile innovation. Korean popular culture achieved unprecedented global dominance through the Korean Wave, with BTS, BLACKPINK, Squid Game, and Parasite captivating international audiences and generating massive cultural influence. South Korea balanced remarkable economic achievements with persistent challenges including gender inequality, intense education competition, and ongoing military tensions with North Korea across the demilitarized zone. Today South Korea stands as a fully developed democracy and high-income nation, yet remains technically at war with the North, with periodic diplomatic initiatives offering tentative hope for eventual reconciliation.
